original_abstract: This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the spatial structure of Polish agriculture based on data from the 2010 Agricultural Census. By evaluating six distinct groups of internal agricultural factors—land quality and use, agrarian structure, socio-demographic characteristics, technical equipment, production structure, and economic performance—the study develops a composite index to measure the general level of agricultural development and structural health. These spatial patterns are then analyzed alongside key external determinants, including natural conditions, historical legacies, urbanization levels, and the distribution of financial support through the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP). The findings reveal that the diverse spatial landscape of Polish agriculture is only minimally influenced by natural environmental factors. Instead, among human-driven determinants, historical regional boundaries remain the most significant factor shaping the industry today, persisting even despite the high absorption of European Union funds.
humanized_abstract: Abstract Agrarian structure in Poland has undergone substantial transformations over recent decades, yet its current spatial structure remains a phenomenon that has not been fully explored. In this paper we endeavour to describe the spatial structure of contemporary Polish agriculture on the basis of the Agricultural Census of 2010. By examining the influence of selected groups of internal agricultural factors on the degree of development of Polish agriculture (including land quality and use, agrarian structure, socio-demographic factors, technical equipment, the structure of agricultural production and the situation in the agricultural household), we derived a composite index that can be considered a synthetic indicator of the state of agriculture. An analysis of the relations between the spatial structure of Polish agriculture and the investigated external factors such as natural factors, historical factors, urbanization and direct support under the CAP was also conducted. It was established that the spatial structure of Polish agriculture is formed under highly diverse conditions and that the natural factors only have an insignificant impact on it. Of the human-caused factors, the most important of the investigated factors that determine the current spatial structure of agriculture are historical structures of particular regions, which –despite the significant absorption of EU support – are still visible and unchanged.
